title: Translation and Interpretation Services

narrative.executive_view: Translation and interpretation services are a heterogeneous, AI-exposed industry rather than a clean cost-savings buy. The exact code includes written translation, speech interpretation and sign-language services [S1]. General written translation is being compressed by AI while health-care, court, regulated and live interpreting work has a demand floor. With only 28 supplied firms in the inferred target EBITDA band, a buyer needs a segment- and contract-specific diligence case, not a generic language-services thesis.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI changes written translation first: drafting, translation-memory work, quality checks, vendor assignment and project administration can be automated or compressed. Nimdzi reports that traditional translation can rise in volume while declining in revenue and says LLM adoption follows a gradual testing/validation path, especially in low-risk work [S2]. Remote/live interpretation, qualified human review and specialized workflows retain more human execution and liability ownership.
narrative.value_capture: Capture is weak for ordinary per-word translation because client procurement can use lower-cost AI-enabled alternatives. It is better for recurring, regulated contracts where a provider owns compliance, scheduling, qualified interpreter supply and operational integration. HHS confirms that covered health programs have language-assistance obligations [S3], but that demand floor is not evidence that an LSP keeps every automation saving; pi_dist therefore remains low and terminal demand is assessed separately.
narrative.adoption_timing: I estimate 40% current effective AI adoption and 1.5 years to 50% for general translation and back-office workflows, not for all revenue. Nimdzi describes four-to-six years for neural MT to become mainstream and a repeating gradual adoption pattern for LLMs [S2]. This estimate separates operational use from a claim that most live interpretation has already been displaced.
narrative.consolidation_economics: Consolidation is real: Slator's 2025 report covered 57 language-industry M&A transactions [S4]. However, current valuations depend on recurring revenue, specialization, process depth, customer concentration and AI exposure. A language-industry M&A adviser reports healthy LSPs at 3-6x EBITDA, with firms under $3m revenue at 2-3.5x [S5]; historic scaled platforms were much dearer, as Teleperformance paid $1.522bn for LanguageLine against $147m adjusted EBITDA in 2016 [S6]. The latter is a non-comparable strategic platform, not a small-target comp.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED. HHS identifies interpreter or document-translation needs in covered health programs and the underlying Title VI/Section 1557 language-access requirements [S3]. That protects a portion of healthcare and similarly regulated live service, but it does not protect routine written translation, localization, or all sign-language and remote modalities from automation and procurement repricing. A blended operator's revenue mix is therefore decisive.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The supplied 4-digit role mix is materially contaminated by veterinary occupations and is not an actual LSP occupational census. I retain every supplied SOC as required but treat their automation fractions as a disclosed proxy rather than evidence about language-company staffing. Language-industry research also spans technology, dubbing, data/AI services and global providers [S7], so it cannot be mapped mechanically to this US NAICS code. [S7]

sources.title: Census NAICS 541930 definition
sources.what_it_supports: The exact NAICS scope includes written translation, speech interpretation and sign-language services, with specified exclusions for transcription and captioning.

sources.title: Preliminary Findings from the 2025 Nimdzi 100
sources.what_it_supports: Nimdzi says neural MT took four to six years to become mainstream, LLM adoption is repeating a gradual validation pattern, and traditional translation volume can increase while revenue declines.

sources.title: HHS Limited English Proficiency
sources.what_it_supports: HHS identifies interpreter/document translation needs and Title VI and Section 1557 language-access requirements for covered programs.

sources.title: Slator Research Hub
sources.what_it_supports: Slator's 2025 language-industry M&A and funding report says it analyzed 57 M&A transactions and 25 funding rounds.

sources.title: What's My Language Company Worth in 2025?
sources.what_it_supports: A language-services M&A adviser states that buyers use EBITDA multiples and reports 3-6x EBITDA for healthy LSPs, with 2-3.5x for firms under $3m revenue.

sources.title: Teleperformance agreement to acquire LanguageLine
sources.what_it_supports: The 2016 transaction announcement reports $1.522bn consideration, $388m revenue and $147m adjusted EBITDA for LanguageLine, a scaled language-access platform.

sources.title: The 2025 Nimdzi 100
sources.what_it_supports: Nimdzi's broad language-services definition includes translation, localization, interpretation, machine translation, data/AI services and generative-AI creation/editing, warning against direct NAICS mapping.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541930 ($2,402,956,156) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541930 ($6,131,619,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Veterinary Assistants and Laboratory Animal Caretakers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $162,427 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541930); class <5: 2,193 firms, avg revenue/firm $268,027 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,946 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 223 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,388,377 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $217,281 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,092,642 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $483,998 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 95 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,796,705 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $907,184 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,892,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,399,598 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $211,163,133 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,047,030 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 28
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Written translation and operational workflows are highly exposed, but the supplied 4-digit occupational mix is not an LSP staffing distribution and live/regulated work remains less automatable.
ai_replaceable_share.plausible_range: 0.40-0.70 of wage share under the contaminated proxy
caveats: The mandated SOC identities are a 4-digit 5419 proxy dominated by sibling industries, including veterinary services.
Freelance linguist cost is often outside W-2 wages, so labor-share V understates the cost base AI attacks.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proxy-mapped to structured production/project tasks; the actual SOC is not an LSP role.

breakdown_by_role.role: Veterinary Assistants and Laboratory Animal Caretakers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proxy-mapped to scheduling and operational coordination; actual SOC mismatch is material.
Proxy-mapped to senior review/compliance oversight; actual SOC mismatch is material.
Reception, intake and routine communication automate heavily.
Unusable industry proxy; assigned a middle fraction rather than asserting LSP evidence.
Scheduling, routing and routine customer-service work are highly automatable with escalation retained.
Capacity planning and reporting automate, while client, quality and compliance accountability remain.
Unusable industry proxy; assigned a middle fraction rather than asserting LSP evidence.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: RFP drafting and quoting automate partly; account ownership and regulated selling persist.
General written translation is highly exposed, while live, certified and high-liability work retains a human role.
Residual is modeled as a mix of linguist, PM, scheduling and back-office work.

pi_dist.rationale: Revenue decline in traditional translation despite volume growth indicates substantial pass-through and repricing risk.
caveats: Regulated interpreting and fixed/recurring contracts can retain more value than general per-word translation.

pi_moat.rationale: Recurring regulated contracts, specialization and operational scale create a modest moat; undifferentiated written translation does not.
caveats: No company-specific contract, credential or customer-concentration data are provided.

t50_years.rationale: Nimdzi's adoption history supports a short but nonzero path for majority effective use in general translation and operations.
caveats: This is not a forecast of 50% replacement of live interpreters or all revenue lines.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: A 40% effective-use estimate recognizes visible LLM uptake while preserving the evidence's gradual-adoption qualification.
caveats: No representative US NAICS 541930 adoption sample was found in reopened sources.

historical_analogs.value: Neural machine translation became mainstream over four to six years; LLM use follows a similar testing-and-validation pattern but expands automation into project and administrative work.
historical_analogs.rationale: The historical analogue informs adoption timing only, not capture, consolidation or terminal demand.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Comparable adoption shape, not an exact duration forecast

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No reopened US LSP owner-age data support a precise estimate.
caveats: Do not interpret this as documented succession evidence.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened source documents a code-specific US succession shortage.
caveats: Absence of an observed source is not proof of no succession need.

active_consolidators.rationale: The Slator transaction count and LanguageLine strategic acquisition support an active buyer universe; three is a conservative count of material buyer types rather than a census.
caveats: The 57 transactions are global and include varied language-industry segments, not a US small-LSP buyer count.

buy_mult.rationale: 3.5x EBITDA is the top of the reopened adviser's under-$3m-revenue band and below its 3-6x healthy-LSP range, reflecting AI-exposed blended revenue.
caveats: S5 is an adviser-published valuation range, not a transaction database; LanguageLine is a large historical strategic-platform comparator, not a small-target comp.

exit_mult.rationale: Exit is modestly lower than entry because AI exposure and general-translation pricing pressure offset consolidation interest absent proof of contract quality.
caveats: No company-specific revenue mix, recurring-contract evidence or recent closed small-LSP transaction is available.

pricing_structure.value: General translation is commonly project/per-word or rate-card based; interpreting is commonly time/encounter based, with regulated contracts as a distinct channel.
pricing_structure.rationale: The source-supported mix of translation and interpreting plus regulated language access supports distinct pricing and capture channels.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Mix varies materially by operator and vertical
caveats: No code-wide US revenue or contract-pricing split is provided by reopened sources.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Fast adoption of general translation workflows is compatible with low retained uplift because customers can demand price reductions; live/regulated service is modeled separately in moat and terminal demand.

terminal_value.justification: Healthcare language-access obligations create a paid demand floor, but general written translation and some remote modalities remain exposed to lower-cost AI alternatives and repricing.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No: the supplied SOC mix is a disclosed contaminated proxy, global language-industry sources are not a US NAICS census, and valuation ranges are adviser evidence rather than closed small-target deals.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Nimdzi's observation that traditional translation can grow in volume while declining in revenue is the direct deflation channel; it supports low pi_dist, not a terminal-demand conclusion. [S2]

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied 4-digit 5419 wage mix is dominated by sibling industries and is not representative of LSP roles; freelance linguist cost may sit in COGS rather than wage labor.
sensitivity: A true LSP occupation mix could increase or decrease V materially.
A full-cost view could show more automatable freelance spend but does not make it available in the frozen wage-share formula.

market_boundary.selection_basis: Census establishes translation, speech interpretation and sign-language scope; Nimdzi confirms industry research extends beyond the exact NAICS boundary.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Earlier majority workflow adoption raises A but does not increase capture in general translation.
A real LSP role mix could materially change V because the frozen SOC proxy is not representative.
Contract mix and AI exposure may compress or support M; no company-specific evidence is available.

reviewer_note: All seven score-driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. The exact NAICS scope, contaminated role proxy, global-source boundary and non-comparable platform multiple are explicitly disclosed.
